Braised Beef Spicy Pork Intestine Spicy Chicken Rice Noodle

Braised Beef Spicy Pork Intestine Spicy Chicken Rice Noodle

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for rice noodle dish with mixed meat toppings; FOODON_00002187 (noodle dish) used as the closest broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked rice noodles as a base; no composite code exists for the full dish with braised beef, pork intestine, chicken, and gizzard toppings. Broader match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite rice noodle dish with mixed meat toppings.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Premium Meat Lover Rice Noodle is approximately 450 g, comprising a generous portion of rice noodles in broth topped with braised beef, spicy pork intestine, spicy diced chicken, and spicy chicken gizzard.

nutrient_source.title: Premium Meat Lover Rice Noodle 豪华版全家福米线 — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Data
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore — Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010490). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (mixed meat rice noodle dish); see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Rice noodles, boiled (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI of 61 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice noodles (GI ~61, medium category), which constitutes the primary carbohydrate vehicle. Mixed meat toppings contribute negligible additional carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (61 × 25.19 g/100g × 450 g serving / 100) / 100 ≈ (61 × 113.36 g) / 100 ≈ 69.1 → corrected: GL = GI × carb_g_per_serving / 100 = 61 × 56.9 / 100 ≈ 34.7. Serving carbohydrate: 25.19 g/100g × 450 g = 113.4 g available carbohydrate; GL = 61 × 113.4 / 100 = 69.2. Note: GL per serving is high due to large bowl size; per-100g GL = 61 × 25.19 / 100 = 15.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g with likely higher actual intake from broth and meat seasonings
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced broth and sauce, as the per-100g sodium of 231 mg is likely underestimated for a full bowl; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg K : 231 mg Na) partially offsets risk, but total bowl sodium may exceed 800–1000 mg and should be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI rice noodle base (GI ~61) with moderate carbohydrate load (25.2 g/100g); dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g provides partial glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 200–250 g to keep glycaemic load manageable, as a full 450 g bowl delivers approximately 113 g of carbohydrate; the 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre moderates but does not eliminate the glycaemic impact of the rice noodle base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Mixed red and organ meats (beef, pork intestine) contribute saturated fat and cholesterol; sodium from broth elevates cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the red meat and organ meat content (braised beef, pork intestine), which contribute saturated fat and dietary cholesterol; requesting a smaller portion, less broth, and substituting chicken-only toppings would reduce saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef, pork) high in arachidonic acid amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways relevant to RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish due to the inclusion of braised beef and pork intestine, both rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies joint inflammation; a chicken-only or seafood variant with reduced broth is preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Premium Meat Lover Rice Noodle (豪华版全家福米线) is a hearty Chinese-Singaporean rice noodle bowl featuring four distinct meat toppings: red-braised beef (红烧牛肉), spicy pork intestine (红烧肥肠), spicy diced chicken (辣子鸡), and spicy chicken gizzard (泡椒鸡杂), served in a savoury broth over a generous bed of smooth, silky rice noodles. The dish is a premium variant of the classic 'quánjiāfú' (全家福, meaning 'whole family happiness') rice noodle format popular in Chinese-Singaporean hawker and restaurant settings. The rice noodles are boiled until tender and have a soft, slightly chewy texture; the braised beef is fork-tender with a rich soy-and-spice flavour; the pork intestine is cleaned, braised, and spiced; the chicken components are wok-tossed with dried chillies and Sichuan-influenced seasonings. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is notable at 3.7 g/100 g, and potassium at 327 mg/100 g exceeds sodium at 231 mg/100 g, which is clinically relevant for blood pressure management. The glycaemic index of the rice noodle base is approximately 61 (medium), making portion control important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The inclusion of red meat and organ meat raises concerns for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis patients due to saturated fat and arachidonic acid content. This dish is suitable for lunch or dinner and is commonly found in Chinese noodle restaurants and hawker centres in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010490, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogy and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice noodles as the closest approved analogous food.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100g) is not reported in the HPB source for this entry; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for a comparable mixed meat rice noodle dish (USDA FDC ID 174289 analogy) and should be verified when HPB updates its record. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is unusual given the meat content and may reflect a reporting limitation; the value is preserved as reported but flagged for verification. 4. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. 5. Glycemic load per serving (34.7) is calculated for a 450 g bowl: GL = 61 × (25.19 × 4.5) / 100 = 61 × 113.36 / 100 ≈ 69.1 for the full bowl; the figure 34.7 represents GL for a half-bowl (225 g) portion more appropriate for clinical guidance. Full-bowl GL ≈ 69. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; pork intestine is a pork product and this dish is not halal. 7. The dish name 全家福 (quánjiāfú) translates literally as 'whole family happiness' and refers to the combination of multiple meat toppings.
